title: Honors Biology: History of Life and Early Earth Review
description: Comprehensive review covering Earth's origin, early atmosphere, dating methods, and the co-evolution of life and the environment.

questions.prompt: Based on the Big Bang theory, what primary observation did Edwin Hubble use to conclude that the universe is currently expanding?
options: The presence of cosmic background radiation across the sky.
The consistent redshift in light from distant celestial galaxies.
The discovery of superdense matter concentrated in one mass.
The varying chemical composition of stars in different galaxies.
questions.explanation: Hubble measured the wavelengths of light from distant galaxies and found they were consistently shifted toward the red end of the spectrum (redshift), indicating they are moving away from Earth[cite: 28, 31].

questions.prompt: If a geologist discovers a zircon crystal in a solidified magma layer, which dating method would be most precise for determining the absolute age of that rock?
options: Measuring the ratio of carbon-14 to carbon-12 in the crystal.
Calculating the decay of potassium-40 into argon-40 gas.
Analyzing the decay of uranium-238 into lead-206 isotopes.
Comparing the crystal structure to known index fossil shapes.
questions.explanation: Uranium is trapped in zircon crystals when magma solidifies, and its decay into lead provides ages with an error of less than 1% for very old materials[cite: 61, 62, 63].

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the transition from Earth’s initial molten state to the formation of its primary internal layers?
options: Centrifugal force pushed heavier elements to the outer crustal layer.
Dense elements like iron sank while lighter elements rose to the top.
Rapid cooling caused all elements to freeze in a homogenous mixture.
Volcanic outgassing pulled the heaviest metals toward the atmosphere.
questions.explanation: When Earth was liquid rock, heavier elements like iron and nickel sank toward the center, while lighter elements collected near the surface to form the crust[cite: 1609, 1610].

questions.prompt: A lab technician is trying to date a bone fragment thought to be 80,000 years old using Carbon-14. Why is this technique likely to fail?
options: Carbon-14 only exists in volcanic rocks rather than organic remains.
The half-life of Carbon-14 is too short for samples older than 50,000 years.
Cosmic rays only began producing Carbon-14 within the last 10,000 years.
Nitrogen-14 interference becomes too high in samples of that specific age.
questions.explanation: The half-life of carbon-14 is 5,730 years; after approximately 50,000 to 60,000 years, too little remains to be measured accurately[cite: 52, 54, 1099].

questions.prompt: What was the primary composition of Earth’s atmosphere during the Hadean Eon before the emergence of photosynthetic life?
options: High concentrations of oxygen, nitrogen, and protective ozone.
Carbon dioxide, water vapor, nitrogen, and hydrogen cyanide.
Methane, ammonia, oxygen, and high levels of noble gases.
Pure hydrogen and helium left over from the original nebula.
questions.explanation: The early atmosphere contained little or no oxygen and was mainly composed of carbon dioxide, water vapor, and nitrogen, along with toxic gases like hydrogen cyanide[cite: 1616, 1434].

questions.prompt: In the Miller-Urey experiment, what specific environmental condition was the 'electric spark' designed to simulate?
options: The high heat produced by the decay of radioactive isotopes.
The intense ultraviolet radiation from the young, active sun.
Lightning storms providing energy for chemical reactions.
The kinetic energy of meteors colliding with the early crust.
questions.explanation: Miller and Urey passed sparks of electricity through their gas mixture to simulate lightning on early Earth[cite: 1153].

questions.prompt: The discovery of Tiktaalik is considered a 'predicted' find in paleontology. What evidence did researchers use to select their dig site?
options: They searched in volcanic regions where fossils are best preserved.
They looked for 375-million-year-old sedimentary rocks in arctic areas.
They used DNA analysis of modern fish to pinpoint the North Pole.
They followed a trail of index fossils found in South American caves.
questions.explanation: Researchers chose the site by studying geology and geography, specifically looking for rocks of the right age (Devonian) where a transitional fossil might exist[cite: 1551, 1566].

questions.prompt: How does the theory of plate tectonics explain the movement of the Earth's lithospheric plates?
options: The plates float on the liquid water of the deep hydrosphere.
Convection currents in the slowly-flowing mantle move the plates.
Changes in Earth's magnetic field pull the iron-rich crustal plates.
The weight of the atmosphere pushes the plates in a southern direction.
questions.explanation: Plate tectonics explains plate movement as a result of convection currents in the mostly solid, yet slowly-flowing mantle[cite: 1435].

questions.prompt: Which eon is characterized by the first appearance of life and the initial release of free oxygen into the atmosphere?
options: The Hadean Eon.
The Archean Eon.
The Proterozoic Eon.
The Phanerozoic Eon.
questions.explanation: Life first appeared during the Archean Eon, which followed the Hadean Eon[cite: 1113].

questions.prompt: When using relative dating, what is the significance of finding the same index fossil in two widely separated rock layers?
options: It indicates the two layers were formed by the same volcano.
It suggests that the two rock layers are likely similar in age.
It proves that the continents were once physically connected.
It shows that the environment in both areas was always desert.
questions.explanation: If the same index fossil is found in two widely separated rock layers, the layers are probably similar in age[cite: 1080].

questions.prompt: Compare the 'RNA World' hypothesis to modern DNA-directed protein synthesis. What is the central claim of the RNA World hypothesis?
options: RNA and DNA evolved simultaneously from simple amino acids.
Proteins were the first molecules to carry genetic information.
RNA existed and could self-replicate before DNA or proteins.
DNA was originally a waste product of RNA-based metabolism.
questions.explanation: The RNA world hypothesis proposes that RNA existed before DNA and led to today's DNA-directed systems[cite: 1443, 1444].

questions.prompt: According to the geologic timescale, which of the following events occurred most recently?
options: The appearance of the first multicellular animals.
The extinction of the dinosaurs at the end of the Mesozoic.
The evolution of the first eukaryotic cells.
The colonization of land by the first vascular plants.
questions.explanation: On a 24-hour clock model of Earth's history, eukaryotic cells appear at 12:48 PM, land plants at 9:28 PM, and dinosaurs go extinct at 11:39 PM[cite: 1131, 1133, 1134].

questions.prompt: What anatomical feature of Tiktaalik provided evidence that it could support its weight against gravity, unlike typical fish?
options: The presence of a streamlined body and a powerful dorsal fin.
Rib bones that attached to one another and a bony pelvis.
A lack of gills and the presence of fully developed lungs.
Lightweight, hollow bones similar to those found in birds.
questions.explanation: Tiktaalik had rib bones that attached to one another and fins connected to the backbone by bony structures like a shoulder and pelvis[cite: 1557, 1559, 1560].

questions.prompt: In a lab simulation of early Earth, you exclude oxygen from the atmosphere. Why is this step historically accurate?
options: Oxygen would have caused the early oceans to evaporate too quickly.
The early atmosphere was formed from volcanic gases, which lack O2.
Early life-forms required hydrogen cyanide as their primary energy.
Oxygen was only introduced to Earth by ice-rich comets later on.
questions.explanation: Earth's early atmosphere was formed by gases escaping from the hot interior (volcanism) and contained little or no oxygen[cite: 84, 1615, 1616].

questions.prompt: Why are divisions of the geologic time scale, like periods and eras, not of equal length?
options: The rate of radioactive decay changes depending on the era.
They are based on major changes in the fossil record and rock layers.
The Earth's rotation was much faster in the Precambrian than today.
Early geologists used a base-10 system that was later abandoned.
questions.explanation: Geologists used rock-layer boundaries and major changes in the fossil record to determine divisions, leading to inconsistent lengths[cite: 1104, 1105, 1106].

questions.prompt: Which of the following describes the 'coevolution' of Earth's systems and life as seen in the color of the sky?
options: The sky turned from blue to pink as carbon dioxide levels rose.
The sky turned from pinkish-orange to blue as life added oxygen.
The sky remained black until the atmosphere became thick with O2.
The sky changed from green to blue when the oceans first formed.
questions.explanation: Early Earth's sky was pinkish-orange due to the atmosphere; as life established and added oxygen, the sky turned blue[cite: 1617, 1480].

questions.prompt: A researcher finds a layer of sedimentary rock sandwiched between two volcanic ash layers. How can they determine the age of fossils in the sediment?
options: By using Carbon-14 on the volcanic ash to find its exact age.
By dating the volcanic layers and inferring the sediment's age.
By measuring the water content of the sedimentary rock layer.
By counting the number of silt grains per square centimeter.
questions.explanation: Fossils in sedimentary rocks can be dated by establishing the ages of the surrounding (sandwiched) volcanic rocks using radiometric methods[cite: 58].

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a key difference between the Hadean and Archean Eons?
options: The Hadean had oceans, while the Archean was entirely molten.
The Archean saw the first life, while the Hadean was pre-life.
The Hadean had an oxygen-rich atmosphere unlike the Archean.
The Archean occurred 15 billion years ago during the Big Bang.
questions.explanation: The Hadean Eon (formation to 4 billion years ago) saw the first rocks, while the Archean Eon is when life first appeared[cite: 1112, 1113].

questions.prompt: What role did gravity play in the formation of the solar system according to the nebular hypothesis?
options: It caused the central sun to explode and create the planets.
It pulled dust and gas together into a rotating, flattened disk.
It pushed the lighter gases toward the center to form the sun.
It prevented any heat from escaping the original nebular cloud.
questions.explanation: Gravity pulled gases and particles together, causing the nebular cloud to contract into a rotating flattened disk[cite: 1595, 1599].

questions.prompt: If you are studying 'macroevolutionary patterns,' which of the following would you be investigating?
options: The change in allele frequency within a single population.
The large-scale transition of species from water to land.
The chemical structure of a single RNA molecule in a lab.
The rate of mutations in a specific bacterial colony.
questions.explanation: Macroevolutionary patterns refer to large-scale changes, such as the evolution of tetrapods from fish[cite: 1471, 1546].

questions.prompt: Which radioactive isotope is most useful for dating materials as old as the solar system (4.6 billion years)?
options: Carbon-14
Potassium-40
Uranium-238
Nitrogen-14
questions.explanation: Uranium-lead dating systems are used for materials from thousands of years old to as old as the solar system[cite: 64].

questions.prompt: What distinguishes the lithosphere from other layers of the Earth's geosphere?
options: It is the only layer composed entirely of liquid iron and nickel.
It consists of the crust and the outermost part of the mantle.
It is the thickest layer, extending 2900 kilometers deep.
It is the layer that generates the most radioactive heat.
questions.explanation: The crust and outermost part of the mantle together form the lithosphere[cite: 1612].

questions.prompt: Based on the geologic time scale, during which era did the first mammals appear?
options: Paleozoic Era
Mesozoic Era
Cenozoic Era
Precambrian Time
questions.explanation: The geologic timescale shows mammals appearing roughly 220 million years ago, which falls within the Mesozoic Era[cite: 46].

questions.prompt: How did the formation of the oceans affect the early Earth's hydrosphere?
options: It removed all nitrogen from the atmosphere through rain.
It allowed water to collect in liquid form on the surface.
It caused the planet's core to cool and become solid iron.
It prevented radioactive decay from heating the interior.
questions.explanation: As Earth cooled, water vapor condensed into rain and eventually collected to form oceans, creating the hydrosphere[cite: 1618, 1620].

questions.prompt: In the context of early Earth, what was the significance of 'stromatolites'?
options: They are the oldest known volcanic rock structures on Earth.
They are structures formed by ancient cyanobacteria.
They are the crystals used for uranium-lead radiometric dating.
They are the first land animals to evolve from lobe-finned fish.
questions.explanation: Stromatolites are structures formed by cyanobacteria, which are early prokaryotic life forms[cite: 1, 46].

questions.prompt: What is the primary source of the internal heat that drives plate tectonics today?
options: Solar radiation absorbed by the Earth's thin outer crust.
Frictional heating from the tides caused by the moon's gravity.
The decay of radioactive elements like uranium and thorium.
Chemical reactions between seawater and the ocean floor.
questions.explanation: The decay of radioactive elements like uranium, thorium, and potassium-40 is the primary source of heat within Earth[cite: 82].

questions.prompt: If a species evolves into several distinct species adapted to different niches, what is this process called?
options: Convergent evolution
Adaptive radiation
Punctuated equilibrium
Coevolution
questions.explanation: Adaptive radiation is the process where a single species evolves into several or many distinct species[cite: 1338].

questions.prompt: What did the Miller-Urey experiment prove about the origin of life?
options: That DNA could be synthesized in a lab within a few days.
That organic compounds could form under early Earth conditions.
That the first living cells were definitely formed in the ocean.
That oxygen was necessary for the first metabolic pathways.
questions.explanation: The experiment suggested that organic compounds necessary for life could have developed from simpler compounds on a primitive Earth[cite: 1442].

questions.prompt: Why is the fossil record often described as 'incomplete' by scientists?
options: Most organisms decay completely without leaving any remains.
The Earth's core has melted most of the fossils from the past.
Only marine organisms have the ability to form fossils in rock.
Fossils can only form in areas with high levels of volcanic ash.
questions.explanation: There are no witnesses and many events are difficult to answer because direct evidence like fossils is rare[cite: 5, 1441].

questions.prompt: Which of the following describes the relationship between 'eons', 'eras', and 'periods'?
options: Periods are the largest, subdivided into eons and then eras.
Eons are the largest, subdivided into eras and then periods.
Eras are the largest, subdivided into periods and then eons.
All three terms refer to the same length of 100 million years.
questions.explanation: The major divisions of the geologic time scale are eons, which are subdivided into eras, and then periods[cite: 1102, 1116, 1117].

questions.prompt: How did the cooling of the Earth affect the formation of the atmosphere?
options: It caused oxygen to be trapped in the solid iron core.
It allowed gases from the interior to form a primitive atmosphere.
It stopped all volcanic activity and atmospheric growth.
It turned the atmosphere from a liquid into a gaseous state.
questions.explanation: As the outer crust cooled, gases escaping from the hot interior formed a primitive atmosphere[cite: 84].

questions.prompt: What evidence indicates that the Moon formed at about the same time as the Earth?
options: Analysis of lunar rocks and comparison with Earth's oldest rocks.
The Moon's atmosphere is identical to Earth's early atmosphere.
The presence of liquid water oceans on the Moon's dark side.
The Moon's redshift matches the redshift of the Earth's sun.
questions.explanation: Analysis of rocks from the Moon and meteorites confirms the estimate that both formed about 4.6 billion years ago[cite: 40, 41, 42].

questions.prompt: Why did the sky of early Earth appear pinkish-orange instead of the blue we see today?
options: The sun was much cooler and emitted only red light waves.
The lack of oxygen and presence of gases like CO2 and nitrogen.
The oceans had not yet formed to reflect the blue color.
Massive amounts of dust from constant meteor impacts.
questions.explanation: Because of gases like carbon dioxide, nitrogen, and hydrogen cyanide, and the lack of oxygen, the sky was probably pinkish-orange[cite: 1616, 1617].

questions.prompt: Compare 'background extinction' and 'mass extinction'. What distinguishes a mass extinction?
options: It only affects a single species in a very small area.
It occurs slowly over billions of years of geologic time.
It affects many species across the entire planet quickly.
It is caused only by human activities like deforestation.
questions.explanation: Mass extinctions are macroevolutionary patterns where many types of living things become extinct at the same time[cite: 1331, 1471].

questions.prompt: In the context of evolution, what is 'convergent evolution'?
options: Two species evolving together in a mutualistic relationship.
Unrelated organisms evolving similar traits in similar environments.
One species splitting into many new species very quickly.
The process of a species becoming extinct due to competition.
questions.explanation: The evolution of similar characteristics in unrelated organisms is known as convergent evolution[cite: 1351].

questions.prompt: What is the 'half-life' of a radioactive isotope?
options: The total time it takes for an entire sample to disappear.
The time required for half of the atoms in a sample to decay.
The age at which a fossil becomes too old to be dated.
The amount of lead produced by one gram of uranium.
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time it takes for half of the radioactive atoms in a sample to decay into stable atoms[cite: 52, 1476].

questions.prompt: Which of the following provided the first evidence of 'humanlike apes' in the geologic record?
options: Cenozoic Era, approximately 7 million years ago.
Mesozoic Era, during the time of the dinosaurs.
Paleozoic Era, alongside the first land plants.
Precambrian Time, before the first eukaryotes.
questions.explanation: The geologic timescale places the first evidence of humanlike apes at 7-5 million years ago in the Cenozoic Era[cite: 46].

questions.prompt: How did the first prokaryotes change the Earth's environment roughly 3.5 billion years ago?
options: They cooled the planet by absorbing all the internal heat.
They began releasing free oxygen into the atmosphere.
They created the first solid rocks through decomposition.
They removed all the nitrogen to create a vacuum.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes began releasing free oxygen into the atmosphere approximately 3.5 billion years ago[cite: 46].

questions.prompt: What geological unit is formed by the combination of the crust and the outermost mantle?
options: The Biosphere
The Lithosphere
The Hydrosphere
The Core
questions.explanation: The crust and outermost part of the mantle together form a geologic unit called the lithosphere[cite: 1612].

questions.prompt: What is a major limitation of using relative dating for fossils?
options: It cannot provide a specific age in years for the fossil.
It only works for fossils found in volcanic ash layers.
It requires the presence of radioactive uranium-238.
It can only be used for fossils of marine organisms.
questions.explanation: Relative dating allows paleontologists to determine whether a fossil is older or younger than others, but not its absolute age in years[cite: 1101, 1440].

questions.prompt: According to the case study, why was Tiktaalik called a 'fish-a-pod'?
options: It had the scales of a fish but the heart of an amphibian.
It represented a stage between fish and four-legged animals.
It was a fish that could fly using modified pectoral fins.
It lived in the ocean but laid eggs on the sandy beaches.
questions.explanation: It was dubbed 'fish-a-pod' to emphasize it represented a stage between lobe-finned fishes and the first land-dwelling amphibians[cite: 1564].

questions.prompt: Which of the following is considered 'absolute dating'?
options: Comparing the position of two rock layers.
Using the decay of radioactive isotopes.
Identifying the presence of index fossils.
Observing the color of the sedimentary rock.
questions.explanation: Radiometric dating (using isotopes) is a form of absolute dating used to assign specific ages to rock layers[cite: 1101, 1109].

questions.prompt: In the 24-hour clock model of Earth's history, at what time did the first living cells appear?
options: 12:00 AM (Midnight)
questions.explanation: The first living cells are estimated to have appeared at approximately 5:30 AM on the 24-hour model[cite: 1132].

questions.prompt: Which gas, common in the modern atmosphere, was largely absent from the early Earth's atmosphere?
options: Nitrogen
Carbon Dioxide
Oxygen
Water Vapor
questions.explanation: Earth's early atmosphere contained little or no oxygen[cite: 1616, 1433].

questions.prompt: What are 'transuranium elements' as described in the context of nuclear reactions?
options: Elements that decay into uranium over billions of years.
Elements with atomic numbers greater than 92.
Naturally occurring elements found in zircon crystals.
Isotopes of carbon used for dating organic remains.
questions.explanation: Elements with atomic numbers greater than 92 are called transuranium elements [Source: Unit 12 - Radioactivity].

questions.prompt: What is the primary characteristic of the 'Phanerozoic Eon'?
options: It is the eon during which the first rocks formed.
It began at the end of the Proterozoic and continues today.
It covers 90 percent of the Earth's entire history.
It is the time when the atmosphere had no oxygen.
questions.explanation: The Phanerozoic Eon began at the end of the Proterozoic and continues to the present[cite: 1115, 1116].

questions.prompt: How does 'weathering' relate to the fossil record?
options: It is the process that creates the bones of animals.
It can break down or chemically change rocks and fossils.
It is required to turn liquid magma into solid crust.
It is the energy source used for radiometric dating.
questions.explanation: Interactions between Earth's systems can break down or chemically change rocks (weathering) at the surface[cite: 1436].

questions.prompt: What does the 'endosymbiotic theory' explain?
options: The origin of the first organic molecules like RNA.
The origin of complex features in eukaryotic cells.
The process of plate tectonics moving the continents.
The way stars and galaxies form from a nebular cloud.
questions.explanation: Evidence from molecular biology supports the theory that complex features of eukaryotic cells evolved through endosymbiosis[cite: 1445, 1481].

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a 'macroevolutionary pattern'?
options: Adaptive radiation
Photosynthesis
Radioactive decay
Redshift of light
questions.explanation: Adaptive radiation is listed as a macroevolutionary pattern[cite: 1471].

questions.prompt: If you are using Potassium-40 to date a rock, what are you measuring?
options: The amount of nitrogen trapped in the zircon crystals.
The ratio of Potassium-40 to its decay product, Argon-40.
The number of half-lives that have passed since 1950.
The temperature of the magma when it first solidified.
questions.explanation: Potassium-argon dating methods measure the decay of Potassium-40 (half-life 1.3 billion years) to date volcanic rocks[cite: 57].
